title: Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs

input_row.title: Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs
input_row.description: VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. After you've created a flow log, you can view and retrieve its data in Amazon CloudWatch Logs. It is recommended that VPC Flow Logs be enabled for packet "Rejects" for VPCs.
input_row.rationale: VPC Flow Logs provide visibility into network traffic that traverses the VPC and can be used to detect anomalous traffic or gain insights during security workflows.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if VPC Flow logs are enabled: From Console: 1. Sign into the management console. 2. Select Services, then select VPC. 3. In the left navigation pane, select Your VPCs. 4. Select a VPC. 5. In the right pane, select the Flow Logs tab. 6. Ensure a Log Flow exists that has Active in the Status column. From Command Line: 1. Run the describe-vpcs command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) to list the VPC networks available in the current AWS region: Internal Only - General aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> --query Vpcs[].VpcId 2. The command output returns the VpcId of VPCs available in the selected region. 3. Run the describe-flow-logs command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) using the VPC ID to determine if the selected virtual network has the Flow Logs feature enabled: aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter "Name=resource-id,Values=<vpc-id>" • If there are no Flow Logs created for the selected VPC, the command output will return an empty list []. 4. Repeat step 3 for other VPCs in the same region. 5. Change the region by updating --region, and repeat steps 1-4 for each region.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to enable VPC Flow Logs: From Console: 1. Sign into the management console. 2. Select Services, then select VPC. 3. In the left navigation pane, select Your VPCs. 4. Select a VPC. 5. In the right pane, select the Flow Logs tab. 6. If no Flow Log exists, click Create Flow Log. 7. For Filter, select Reject. 8. Enter a Role and Destination Log Group. 9. Click Create Log Flow. 10. Click on CloudWatch Logs Group. Note: Setting the filter to "Reject" will dramatically reduce the accumulation of logging data for this recommendation and provide sufficient information for the purposes of breach detection, research, and remediation. However, during periods of least privilege security group engineering, setting the filter to "All" can be very helpful in discovering existing traffic flows required for the proper operation of an already running environment. From Command Line: 1. Create a policy document, name it role_policy_document.json, and paste the following content: Internal Only - General { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "test", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } 2. Create another policy document, name it iam_policy.json, and paste the following content: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action":[ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:DescribeLogGroups", "logs:DescribeLogStreams", "logs:PutLogEvents", "logs:GetLogEvents", "logs:FilterLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } 3. Run the following command to create an IAM role: aws iam create-role --role-name <aws-support-iam-role> --assume-role-policy-document file://<file-path>role_policy_document.json 4. Run the following command to create an IAM policy: aws iam create-policy --policy-name <iam-policy-name> --policy-document file://<file-path>iam-policy.json Internal Only - General 5. Run the attach-group-policy command, using the IAM policy ARN returned from the previous step to attach the policy to the IAM role: aws iam attach-group-policy --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::<aws-account-id>:policy/<iam-policy-name> --group-name <group-name> • If the command succeeds, no output is returned. 6. Run the describe-vpcs command to get a list of VPCs in the selected region: aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> • The command output should return a list of VPCs in the selected region. 7. Run the create-flow-logs command to create a flow log for a VPC: aws ec2 create-flow-logs --resource-type VPC --resource-ids <vpc-id> --traffic-type REJECT --log-group-name <log-group-name> --deliver-logs-permission-arn <iam-role-arn> 8. Repeat step 7 for other VPCs in the selected region. 9. Change the region by updating --region, and repeat the remediation procedure for each region.
input_row.impact: By default, CloudWatch Logs will store logs indefinitely unless a specific retention period is defined for the log group. When choosing the number of days to retain, keep in mind that the average time it takes for an organization to realize they have been breached is 210 days (at the time of this writing). Since additional time is required to research a breach, a minimum retention policy of 365 days allows for detection and investigation. You may also wish to archive the logs to a cheaper storage service rather than simply deleting them. See the following AWS resource to manage CloudWatch Logs retention periods: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/DeveloperGuide/SettingLogRetention.html

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_vpc_flow_log_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the **AWS CLI command**: `aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> --query Vpcs[].VpcId` to list all VPCs in the selected AWS region.
- For each VPC ID retrieved, run the **AWS CLI command**: `aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter "Name=resource-id,Values=<vpc-id>"` to determine if flow logs are enabled for that specific VPC.
- Alternatively, use **boto3 SDK methods**:
  - Use `ec2.describe_vpcs()` to list VPCs.
  - For each VPC, utilize `ec2.describe_flow_logs(Filters=[{'Name': 'resource-id', 'Values': [vpc_id]}])` to check if flow logs are present and active.
- The expected configuration properties/settings to validate include:
  - Check if the response from `describe_flow_logs` returns a list that is **not empty**, indicating that flow logs are enabled.
  - Ensure that the flow log is associated with the correct VPC and has an "Active" status.
- **Pass/Fail criteria**:
  - Pass: If the VPC has at least one flow log entry with "Active" status.
  - Fail: If the VPC has no flow logs or if none are active. 

This approach allows the security audit of VPC flow logging to be conducted programmatically across multiple regions and VPCs efficiently, ensuring compliance with the defined CIS control.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification as Automated is correct. The task of checking VPC flow logs can be efficiently automated using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI, which aligns with the capabilities of these tools to query AWS resources programmatically.
- The use of AWS CLI and boto3 SDK is appropriate for this task, as both provide the necessary functionality to list VPCs and check for flow logs.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The AWS CLI commands and boto3 methods mentioned are technically correct and suitable for the task. The `describe-vpcs` and `describe-flow-logs` commands/methods are valid and will retrieve the necessary information.
- The criteria for checking if the flow logs are enabled and active are correctly outlined.
- The program name `aws_vpc_flow_log_enabled` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to indicate the focus on "Rejects" or "Active" status.
- The audit approach does not explicitly mention checking for "Rejects" in the flow logs, which is a key part of the CIS control description.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Missing Considerations:** The audit should ensure that the flow logs are configured to capture "Rejects" as specified in the CIS control. This can be checked by examining the `TrafficType` attribute of the flow logs.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** A more descriptive program name could be `aws_vpc_flow_log_active_rejects_enabled`, indicating the focus on active flow logs specifically capturing rejected traffic.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using AWS Config rules such as `vpc-flow-logs-enabled` which can automatically evaluate the compliance of VPC flow logs across regions.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The approach and technical details are mostly accurate, and the use of AWS CLI and boto3 is appropriate for this task. The only significant oversight is the lack of focus on the "Rejects" aspect of the flow logs.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the audit approach to include verification that the flow logs are capturing "Rejects" by checking the `TrafficType` attribute.
- Update the program name to be more descriptive, such as `aws_vpc_flow_log_active_rejects_enabled`, to clearly convey the specific compliance check being performed.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the task of ensuring VPC flow logging is enabled can be automated using AWS CLI and boto3 SDK. The AWS APIs provide the necessary methods to list VPCs and check for flow logs, making it feasible to automate this compliance check. The second AI opinion suggests a more descriptive program name to include the focus on "Rejects" and "Active" status, which is a valid enhancement to ensure the control's requirements are fully met.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
aws_vpc_flow_log_active_rejects_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: VPC Flow Logs
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `aws ec2 describe-vpcs --region <region> --query Vpcs[].VpcId`
  - `aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter "Name=resource-id,Values=<vpc-id>"`
- Properties to Check: 
  - Ensure the response from `describe-flow-logs` is not empty.
  - Check that the flow log has an "Active" status.
  - Verify the `TrafficType` attribute is set to "Reject".
- Pass Criteria: 
  - The VPC has at least one flow log entry with "Active" status and `TrafficType` set to "Reject".
- Fail Criteria: 
  - The VPC has no flow logs, or none are active, or the `TrafficType` is not set to "Reject".

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach, but the second opinion provided a more descriptive program name and highlighted the need to check for the "Reject" traffic type, which was incorporated into the final decision.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Amazon_Web_Services_Foundations_Benchmark_v4.0.1
